item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-11424: Wow. It's like I'm in Middle Earth.
Command: Excuse me?
D-11424: Well, everywhere I look there's just Mordor.

I lol'd

a standard communications

You seem to have accidentally a word.

in the middle of the room there is a a structure resembling a table

Who dare disturb me from my slumber?

The door could just not speak English well, but I think "dares" sounds better.

Ultimately, it's not a bad article, but I just don't like the informal tone and characterization of D-11424 and Command, especially the leniency they treat him with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You waited for 11 just to have a "Dork" Door pun, didn't you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmmmm. This had a lot of things I like… having D-Class who live long enough to be valued specialists (and also keep living!) is great. Please keep doing this. Some of the examples of the doorknob testing like the boulder were interesting, and I liked the visuals and dialogue for a lot of the exploration.

Ultimately, though, it's just a really boring object. Nothing in the exploration really changed that feeling.

-1 I guess

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also mine was gold and fancier.

So snippy! xD A beautiful character beat…for a door. Good stuff all around!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's an awesome idea! I hope so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same with any army, or indeed any organisation, really. Repeatedly training up fresh generations of recruits is tedious, expensive, and saps operational efficiency… especially if there aren't any battle hardened veterans to perform the valuable job of peer-tutoring the newbies in all the little tricks of the trade that aren't covered by Basic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bring me every door

+ show block
– hide block

0079: an AI trapped within a non-networked, early 80s computer. The sort of thing that early windowing systems, including MS Windows, were created on. The world it lives in is essentially a pocket dimension with no route in or out, though it can communicate via the TV and keyboard.
1179: a hole in the ground, which could be called a portal into hell, with a fire demon in it; the internal dimensions are non-euclidian but appear to be bound within a certain 3-dimensional area of baseline reality… there's a word for that. Generally if you enter, you don't leave, because the demon kills you.
2279: a doorway into a somewhat deadly pet shop, where pets you have killed (intentionally or otherwise) are brought back from the dead to sap your vital essence and then erase you from existence. If you're targeted, you're drawn to walk in through it, and then are barred from leaving through that one single access point until you've accepted the animal to take home with you. Non targeted people can freely enter and exit, but are not compelled to do so. The building is an otherwise competely enclosed, impenetrable space.
3379: a transdimensional door lurking under the arctic ocean, leading to a realm of multiple small pocket dimensions (the sky is mentioned as appearing to have a hard border) connected by further similar portals. Exiting the realm is relatively straightforward if you can retrace your steps to the entry point, but it would appear that it ejects you anyway once it's taken its somewhat literal pound of flesh.
4479: a device that can turn any door-like object into a portal to a pocket dimension almost completely composed of doors, or the componentry of the door leading to it, and that is itself full of endless non-Euclidian spaces connected by doors of all different kinds of form, some which can only be opened by using the device. It's very easy to get lost within the space even if using a return tether. However, if you survive travelling forwards through it long enough, you get deposited back at the start, suggesting some kind of exotic multidimensional-toroid topology.

It'll be interesting to see how 5579, 6679 etc carry on this obvious thematic escalation…


EV-ERY-DOOR

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Essentially my headcanon, with the caveat that they never actually terminated them en masse at any point.

An experienced, cooperative D-class who's past his first rodeo is more useful to the Foundation than a terrified fresh meat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe the foundation is starting to run a little short of "recruits", and so are becoming a little more lenient in terms of no longer routinely terminating them at the end of the month … instead "reprocessing" them with amnestics, or simply keeping the more cooperative and useful ones - like 11424 - around to leverage their talents and experience without having to put too many junior researchers in the firing line any more… Thus they have more opportunity to develop beyond being baffled, terrified and/or angry randoms plucked out of various prisons around the world and disposed of before they even properly realise what's going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit, this is where I'd meant to use that "thinking with doortals" crack, rather than on 3379. The whole diving braindump shortcircuited a bit of my memory. Oh well, too late now. And I suppose they're kind of related, like it could be the same anomaly / transdimensional technology that lies behind both of them, just in this case it's gone a little bit wild.

Very clever overall and particularly in the interpretation of the clichés, and it manages to actually avoid feeling clichéd.

Just to clarify, the knob literally attaches to surfaces as if it's weakly magnetic, and removing it is a fairly easy process where you just slide/twist it off said surface, or hold the "door" still and pull slightly hard on the knob (so maybe have to be careful how much force is used to open it normally)? And the tether was freed up from being jammed in the revolving door once the door was smashed/broken down?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m enjoying this new trope of D-classes with character and personality beyond saying “Doc” all the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This whole skip is like that scene from Being John Malkovich, but with doors. Yes, you know the one.

I liked this, but I felt the exploration log was a bit too long and got too silly by the end (the appearance of DOOR-K — yes, I get the pun — nearly turned my upvote into a no-vote). I'd like to see this trimmed down a lot, but as it is, it's just engaging enough to nudge me into an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3379*, and, selfishly because I know Kirby's ego can take it, thank you >:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea if this is a sequel or spiritual successor to SCP-3379, but I love it either way. +1

doors,
lots of doors

3379
4479

you sneaky little boy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The dialogue coming from Command is oddly informal? I read 3397 and this one back-to-back. I thought Dr. Arceo's voice of calmness and sense was a good contrast to D-11424's attitude. Command's dialogue in this one seems a little more forced and diminishes the impact of D-11424 for me. Maybe if it was another doctor's name?
Still love it, but I'd have to put it second to the exploration of 3397.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's time to play "which puns were put in by Kirby and which by weryllium"

item.title: Re: Doors
item.content:encoded: I had a great time working with Kirby on this. Again, teaming up with him was not something I'd expect to do, but I'm glad I did and I'm happy with the result, and I hope it opens the door to more fun collabs with friends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mordor

fuck you Kirby, weryllium wouldn't do this to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can't go wrong with a LoTR pun :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this has better dialogue then 3379 imo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Everywhere I look I see Mordor."

+1 just for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this related to SCP-3311?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: life has many doors ed boy

item.title: Re: Doors
item.content:encoded:
Cryptic Interview Followed By Interviewer Suffering

HAHAHAHA. Best use of this trope I've seen.

Very enjoyable scip, +1.

Edit: for the record, this is a Category 3 entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: now this is how to enter a contest!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article really Lights My Fire

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Must've been a pretty eye-opening experience for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'all make a good combo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the door shaped door to the door doormension

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D O O R S

I couldn't help imagining the closet door facility from Monster's Inc. during this

Also, how'd poor Researcher Mendoza react to their eye becoming a door?

item.title: Doors
item.content:encoded:
Doors.

This is a collab between Weryllium and I, which I never thought I'd be saying to be honest. But it was a blast watching our different styles work together to make something just batshit crazy and fun. 10/10 wery recommend.

Alright, so now formalities. We are incorporating the following clichés:

  • Door To Another World
  • Thing That Does A Thing Really Well
  • Cryptic Interview Followed By Interviewer Suffering

I'd also like to thank mayoculpa, rumetzen, PeppersGhost, DarkStuff, and MaliceAForethought for critiquing this.

Image attribution:

Doorknob: Cooper Hewitt, Smithsonian Design Museum [Public domain]

Hallway: picture taken by Weryllium

Doors.
